icd_category: C71 — Malignant neoplasm of brain (glioblastoma multiforme)
one_liner: A 62-year-old man presents with 6 weeks of progressively worsening morning headaches, new-onset left-sided weakness, confusion, and a generalized tonic-clonic seizure today.

stage2.hpi_extension: For the past 6 weeks he has had a dull, progressive headache that is worst on waking in the morning and improves through the day, sometimes with nausea and one episode of vomiting. His wife has noticed that over the same period he has become uncharacteristically apathetic and forgetful, has had word-finding difficulty, and has been dragging his left foot. This morning he had a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure at breakfast and was brought in by EMS. He has never had seizures before. No fever, no recent head trauma, no known malignancy, no travel, no immunosuppression.
stage2.family_history: Father with colon cancer at age 70; no known brain tumors
stage2.social_history: Retired engineer. Former smoker (quit 15 years ago, 20 pack-years). Social alcohol.
stage2.physical_exam: Postictal, somnolent but arousable. Oriented to self only. Mild expressive aphasia. Funduscopy: bilateral papilledema with blurred disc margins. Cranial nerves otherwise intact. Left hemiparesis (3/5 in the left arm and leg) with increased tone and hyperreflexia on the left; positive Babinski on the left. Sensation decreased to light touch on the left. No meningismus. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams unremarkable. No lymphadenopathy, no breast or skin lesions.

lab_results.result: MRI brain with and without gadolinium: large heterogeneous ring-enhancing mass centered in the right frontal lobe, crossing the corpus callosum to involve the left frontal lobe ('butterfly' pattern), with central necrosis, surrounding vasogenic edema, and mass effect with midline shift. No other lesions.
lab_results.clinical_significance: A ring-enhancing lesion crossing the corpus callosum ('butterfly glioma') with central necrosis in an older adult is highly characteristic of glioblastoma multiforme (WHO grade IV astrocytoma). Differential includes primary CNS lymphoma, brain abscess, and metastasis, but the imaging pattern and clinical context are classic for GBM.

lab_results.result: Stereotactic needle biopsy: highly cellular astrocytic tumor with marked pleomorphism, brisk mitotic activity, pseudopalisading necrosis, and microvascular (endothelial/glomeruloid) proliferation. GFAP positive. MGMT promoter hypermethylated. IDH wild-type. Diagnosis: glioblastoma multiforme, WHO grade IV.
lab_results.clinical_significance: The histologic combination of pseudopalisading necrosis plus microvascular proliferation in a GFAP-positive astrocytic tumor is diagnostic of GBM (WHO grade IV). MGMT promoter methylation predicts better response to temozolomide.
Excludes metabolic precipitants for the seizure and establishes baseline prior to chemoradiation.

stage5.final_clue: He is started on dexamethasone for peritumoral edema and levetiracetam for seizure prophylaxis. He undergoes maximal safe surgical resection followed by concurrent external-beam radiation and temozolomide (Stupp protocol). Despite treatment, his median expected survival is ~14 months.

debrief.classic_presentation: Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM) is the most common primary malignant brain tumor in adults, typically presenting between ages 45 and 70. Classic clinical features reflect a rapidly growing supratentorial mass: progressive headaches (classically worse on waking in the morning, improved as the day progresses), nausea and vomiting, focal neurologic deficits (hemiparesis, aphasia), personality/behavioral change, new-onset seizures, and signs of increased intracranial pressure (papilledema). On MRI, GBM is a heterogeneous ring-enhancing mass with central necrosis and surrounding edema, and it notoriously crosses the corpus callosum to involve both hemispheres, producing the classic 'butterfly glioma' appearance.
debrief.pathophysiology: GBM is a WHO grade IV astrocytoma, arising from astrocytes or glial precursor cells. It is highly infiltrative and rapidly proliferative. Molecular subtypes include IDH wild-type (primary/de novo GBM, the most common in older adults) and IDH-mutant (secondary GBM, arising from a lower-grade astrocytoma). Histopathology is characterized by pseudopalisading necrosis (tumor cells arranged around areas of necrosis), microvascular (endothelial/glomeruloid) proliferation, and marked nuclear pleomorphism. Immunohistochemistry: GFAP positive, reflecting astrocytic origin. MGMT promoter methylation silences the DNA repair enzyme O6-methylguanine-DNA methyltransferase and predicts better response to alkylating chemotherapy (temozolomide).
debrief.treatment_overview: Standard of care is the Stupp protocol: maximal safe surgical resection, followed by concurrent external-beam radiotherapy and temozolomide (oral alkylating agent), followed by adjuvant temozolomide. Dexamethasone is used to reduce peritumoral vasogenic edema and relieve mass effect. Antiepileptics are given for seizures. Tumor-treating fields (TTFields) may be added. Despite aggressive multimodal therapy, prognosis is dismal — median survival is approximately 14-15 months, and 5-year survival is under 10%. Primary CNS lymphoma is an important differential, especially in immunosuppressed patients, and should be excluded before steroid use because steroids can cause tumor regression and obscure the biopsy.
high_yield_pearls: GBM = most common primary malignant brain tumor in adults; WHO grade IV astrocytoma; GFAP positive.
Classic MRI: heterogeneous ring-enhancing mass with central necrosis crossing the corpus callosum ('butterfly glioma').
Histology: pseudopalisading necrosis + microvascular (endothelial/glomeruloid) proliferation are diagnostic.
Standard therapy: maximal safe resection + radiation + temozolomide (Stupp protocol); median survival ~14 months.
Red flags for brain tumor include morning headache that improves through the day, new seizure in an adult, focal deficits, and papilledema.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Neurology and Special Senses chapter, 'Adult primary brain tumors — Glioblastoma multiforme' section.
